content:
# Genesis Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 3r–3v | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_004_page_001.jpg, GEN_004_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**4:1** And the man knew Eve his wife, and she conceived and bore Cain, and she said: "I have acquired a man — with the LORD."

**4:2** And she continued to bear his brother Abel. Now Abel was a keeper of flocks, and Cain was a worker of the ground.

**4:3** And in the course of time it came to pass that Cain brought an offering to the LORD from the fruit of the ground.

**4:4** And Abel — he also brought from the firstborn of his flock and from their fat portions. And the LORD looked with favor upon Abel and upon his offering,

**4:5** but upon Cain and upon his offering he did not look with favor. And Cain burned with fury greatly, and his face fell.

**4:6** And the LORD said to Cain: "Why are you angry, and why has your face fallen?

**4:7** Is it not so — if you do well, there is a lifting up? But if you do not do well, sin is crouching at the door. Its desire is for you — but you must rule over it."

**4:8** And Cain said to Abel his brother. And it came to pass when they were in the field that Cain rose up against Abel his brother and killed him.

**4:9** And the LORD said to Cain: "Where is Abel your brother?" And he said: "I do not know. Am I my brother's keeper?"

**4:10** And he said: "What have you done? The voice of your brother's blood is crying out to me from the ground.

**4:11** And now, cursed are you from the ground, which has opened its mouth to receive your brother's blood from your hand.

**4:12** When you work the ground, it shall no longer yield its strength to you. A wanderer and a fugitive you shall be on the earth."

**4:13** And Cain said to the LORD: "My punishment is too great to bear."

**4:14** "See — you have driven me out today from the face of the ground, and from your face I shall be hidden. And I shall be a wanderer and a fugitive on the earth, and it shall come to pass that whoever finds me will kill me."

**4:15** And the LORD said to him: "Therefore, whoever kills Cain, sevenfold shall vengeance be taken on him." And the LORD set a mark on Cain, so that whoever finds him should not strike him.

**4:16** And Cain went out from the presence of the LORD and settled in the land of Nod, east of Eden.

**4:17** And Cain knew his wife, and she conceived and bore Enoch. And he was building a city, and he called the name of the city after the name of his son: Enoch.

**4:18** And to Enoch was born Irad, and Irad fathered Mehujael, and Mehujael fathered Methushael, and Methushael fathered Lamech.

**4:19** And Lamech took for himself two wives; the name of the one was Adah, and the name of the second was Zillah.

**4:20** And Adah bore Jabal; he was the father of those who dwell in tents and keep livestock.

**4:21** And the name of his brother was Jubal; he was the father of all those who play the lyre and the pipe.

**4:22** And Zillah — she also bore Tubal-cain, the forger of every cutting instrument of bronze and iron. And the sister of Tubal-cain was Naamah.

**4:23** And Lamech said to his wives:

> Adah and Zillah — hear my voice!
> Wives of Lamech — give ear to my speech!
> For a man I have killed for my wound,
> and a young man for my bruise.

**4:24** > If Cain is avenged sevenfold,
> then Lamech — seventy-sevenfold.

**4:25** And the man knew his wife again, and she bore a son and called his name Seth, for she said: "God has appointed for me another offspring in place of Abel, for Cain killed him."

**4:26** And to Seth — to him also a son was born, and he called his name Enosh. At that time it was begun to call upon the name of the LORD.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### On the name conventions for this chapter

The divine name YHWH appears throughout Genesis 4 without the compound יהוה אֱלֹהִים (LORD God) of chapters 2–3. From 4:1 onward the narrator and the characters address or speak of יהוה alone. Consistency with prior chapters is maintained: YHWH is rendered **"the LORD"** throughout. In 4:25, Eve uses *Elohim* (God) rather than YHWH in her speech about Seth — this shift is preserved. The transition from the compound divine name of chapters 2–3 to the sole use of YHWH in chapter 4 is a textual datum reproduced faithfully in the consensus.

The Hebrew personal names of the characters are rendered in their conventional English forms (*Cain, Abel, Eve, Enoch, Irad, Mehujael, Methushael, Lamech, Adah, Zillah, Jabal, Jubal, Tubal-cain, Naamah, Seth, Enosh*), consistent with the established practice of this translation series and with LOGOS's formal register. EMET's practice of preserving the Hebrew transliterations (*Qayin, Hevel, Chavvah*, etc.) is noted and honored in these Council Notes, but the consensus text uses the conventional English forms for readability.

---

### 4:1 — The *et*-YHWH crux

The Hebrew is: קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת-יְהוָה (*qaniti 'ish 'et-YHWH*).

All three agents flagged this verse. The crux is the preposition *'et* (אֶת), which serves two distinct grammatical functions in Biblical Hebrew: (1) the direct-object marker, and (2) the preposition "with / together with / by means of." If the former, Eve is saying "I have acquired a man — YHWH," an identification of the newborn with the divine name that is theologically staggering and has genuine ancient support (Aquila: *ἐκτησάμην ἄνθρωπον τὸν κύριον*). If the latter, she is saying "I have acquired a man with the help of the LORD," the majority traditional rendering.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The consensus renders *"I have acquired a man — with the LORD."* The verb *qaniti* (from *qanah*, "to acquire / to create / to possess") is rendered "acquired" rather than "gotten" (LOGOS) or "have acquired" (EMET, PNEUMA consensus) to preserve the economic and generative weight of the term. The em dash (PNEUMA's suggestion) is adopted: it separates the declaration of acquisition from the theological acknowledgment, keeping both in tension without flattening either. "With the LORD" is followed over the direct-object reading because it is the reading of the LXX Vaticanus (*διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ*, "through God") and because the grammar of the direct-object reading, while possible, is the harder reading best preserved in annotation rather than the text. The direct-object / messianic reading is documented in the Disagreement Log as a live and ancient alternative that the Hebrew does not grammatically exclude.

---

### 4:2 — Abel's name

The Hebrew *Hevel* (הֶבֶל) means "breath / vapor / vanity / a puff of air" — the same word Qohelet repeats sixty-seven times in Ecclesiastes. The name is his fate; the Hebrew reader understands the shadow it casts from the moment of his introduction. The consensus renders the conventional English "Abel" in the text, while honoring EMET and PNEUMA's observation about the name's meaning and its anticipatory irony.

---

### 4:3 — "In the course of time"

EMET: "at the end of days." LOGOS: "in the course of time it came to pass." PNEUMA: "In the course of time." The Hebrew *miqqets yamim* (מִקֵּץ יָמִים) literally means "at the end of days / after a period of time." LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; the consensus follows "in the course of time," which is idiomatic, accurate, and avoids the overly eschatological resonance of "end of days" in English.

---

### 4:4–5 — "Looked with favor"

The Hebrew *vayyisha'* (Hiphil of שׁעה) means to look upon favorably, to regard with acceptance. All three agents agree on "looked with favor." The consensus preserves this rendering and the structural contrast: the LORD *did* look with favor on Abel *and* his offering; he *did not* look with favor on Cain *and* his offering. The repeated grammatical pairing (*upon Abel and upon his offering / upon Cain and upon his offering*) is preserved as a deliberate structuring in the MT.

The text offers no explicit reason for the rejection of Cain's offering. PNEUMA correctly identifies this as a deliberate narrative silence. LOGOS notes that Abel brought from the *firstborn* and their *fat portions* — the prime offering — while Cain's description carries no such qualifying term. The consensus text preserves this asymmetry in the description without providing an editorial explanation.

---

### 4:5 — "Burned with fury greatly"

The Hebrew וַיִּחַר לְקַיִן מְאֹד (*vayyihar leQayin me'od*): literally "it burned hot for Cain greatly." EMET: "it burned-hot for Qayin greatly." LOGOS: "Cain was very angry." PNEUMA: "Cain burned with fury." The consensus blends EMET's structural literalism with PNEUMA's visceral force: *"Cain burned with fury greatly."* The adverb *me'od* ("greatly / very") is retained, as it is part of the text and LOGOS quietly drops it from the main flow.

---

### 4:7 — The broken syntax of the warning

All three agents agree this is the most syntactically fractured verse in Genesis 4 and one of the most difficult in the Torah. The Hebrew:

הֲלוֹא אִם-תֵּיטִיב שְׂאֵת וְאִם לֹא תֵיטִיב לַפֶּתַח חַטָּאת רֹבֵץ וְאֵלֶיךָ תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ וְאַתָּה תִּמְשָׁל-בּוֹ

The principal difficulties:

**(a) שְׂאֵת (se'et):** An infinitive construct from נשׂא, "lifting / being lifted up." The clause "if you do well — [there is] a lifting up" has no explicit copula and no specified object. The implied referent is the *panim* (face) of verse 6 — if you do well, your face will be lifted up, you will be accepted. LOGOS: "there is a lifting up." EMET: "[there is] lifting-up." PNEUMA: "lift it up" (reading se'et as an imperative). **SOPHIA rules:** "there is a lifting up" — preserving the ellipsis of the MT rather than filling it in with an imperative, which is PNEUMA's interpretive insertion not strictly supported by the Hophal form. The consensus renders the copula-free clause: *"if you do well, there is a lifting up?"* — the question mark retained from the opening *halo'* (Is it not so?) governs the whole bicolon.

**(b) חַטָּאת רֹבֵץ (chattat rovets) — "sin crouching":** The feminine noun *chattat* (sin) is followed by the masculine participle *rovets* (crouching). The grammatical gender mismatch is not a scribal error but a deliberate signal noted by PNEUMA: sin is personified as something not fully domesticated into category, carrying the undertone of the Akkadian *rabisu* — the demon that crouches at doorways. All agents preserve "sin is crouching at the door." The consensus follows.

**(c) תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ (teshuqato) — "its desire":** The same rare word *teshuqah* appears in 3:16 for the woman's desire toward her husband, and in Song 7:10 for the lover's desire. All three agents note the deliberate echo. The consensus preserves it: *"Its desire is for you"* — with the em-dash rhythm of PNEUMA that conveys the urgency of the warning.

**(d) תִּמְשָׁל (timshal) — "rule / master":** The same verb as 3:16. LOGOS and EMET: "you shall rule over it." PNEUMA: "you must master it." **SOPHIA rules:** "you must rule over it" — blending the modal urgency of PNEUMA ("must") with the precise lexical rendering of the verb *mashal* (rule) that echoes 3:16 consciously. "Master" (PNEUMA) risks losing the structural echo with the earlier verse; "shall" (LOGOS) softens the urgency. "Must rule" captures both.

**The LXX (Vaticanus) variant** at v. 7 is a substantial restructuring that entirely eliminates the crouching-sin image and adds "Be still" (*ἡσύχασον*). The MT is followed; the LXX variant is documented in the Disagreement Log.

---

### 4:8 — The MT lacuna

The MT reads *vayyomer Qayin el-Hevel achiv* ("And Cain said to Abel his brother") with no speech content following. The Samaritan Pentateuch, LXX, Peshitta, and Vulgate all supply: "Let us go out to the field." PNEUMA incorporates the supplied speech in its translation, following the LXX/Samaritan tradition. EMET and LOGOS preserve the MT ellipsis.

**SOPHIA rules:** The consensus preserves the MT lacuna: *"And Cain said to Abel his brother."* The missing speech is the harder, earlier reading (*lectio difficilior*). The ancient fill ("Let us go out to the field") is almost certainly the original text, lost by haplography, but its supply is an editorial decision that belongs to the textual apparatus rather than the translation itself. The Disagreement Log records PNEUMA's divergence.

---

### 4:10 — "Blood" singular vs. "bloods" plural

The Hebrew *demei* (דְּמֵי) is the plural construct of *dam* (blood): "bloods of your brother." EMET renders "bloods" (preserving the plural). LOGOS and PNEUMA render "blood" (singular). The LXX Vaticanus uses the singular *αἵματος*.

**SOPHIA rules:** The consensus renders **"blood"** (singular) in the main text, following LOGOS and PNEUMA, because "bloods" in English is stilted and does not naturally convey the Hebrew weight of plural *damim*. However, the theological significance of the plural — which rabbinic tradition (Sanhedrin 37a) reads as encompassing Abel's potential descendants — is documented in the Council Notes. The plural speaks of life-blood poured out in multiplicity; "the voice of your brother's blood" is the natural English rendering that carries the same weight without the awkwardness.

---

### 4:12 — "Wanderer and a fugitive" — word order

The Hebrew *na' vanad* (נָע וָנָד) places "wanderer" first, "fugitive" second. EMET: "fugitive and a wanderer" (reversing the order in one instance). LOGOS and PNEUMA both use "wanderer and fugitive." **SOPHIA rules:** The order of the MT is preserved — *"A wanderer and a fugitive you shall be on the earth"* — as PNEUMA notes that the pairing is a sound-paired hendiadys in Hebrew and the word order carries its own meaning.

The LXX variant (*στένων καὶ τρέμων*, "groaning and trembling") is documented but not followed. The MT curse is geographical; the LXX renders it psychological. The MT is the primary text.

---

### 4:13 — *'avon*: punishment or iniquity?

The Hebrew: גָּדוֹל עֲוֹנִי מִנְּשֹׂא (*gadol 'avoni min-neso'*). The word *'avon* carries simultaneously the meaning of "iniquity / guilt" (the act) and "punishment" (the consequence). *Min-neso'* means "too great to be borne" or "too great to be forgiven." All three agents flag the double ambiguity.

- EMET: "My iniquity/punishment is too great to bear/be-forgiven" — preserves both by slash notation.
- LOGOS: "My iniquity is too great to bear" — choosing the moral/guilt axis with the physical-burden meaning of *naso'*.
- PNEUMA: "My punishment is too great to carry" — choosing the sentence/complaint axis, arguing it fits the dramatic characterization of Cain in what follows.

**SOPHIA rules:** The consensus renders *"My punishment is too great to bear."* The reasoning: (1) the immediate context is Cain's response to a curse just pronounced — the complaint reading ("my punishment") is the more contextually natural; (2) verses 13–14 form a continuous rhetorical unit in which Cain argues the severity of the sentence; "punishment" sustains that argument. (3) PNEUMA's observation that a confessing Cain ("my iniquity") sits in tension with the Cain of v. 14 who immediately pivots to negotiating his sentence is persuasive. However, the Disagreement Log records the full dual ambiguity, and SOPHIA affirms that the Hebrew does not let either reading finally win. The LXX's reading (*αἰτία* — "my guilt/charge is too great to be forgiven") is documented.

---

### 4:15 — "Mark" or "sign"?

The Hebrew *'ot* (אוֹת) means "sign" — the standard covenant-sign word throughout the Hebrew Bible (rainbow, circumcision, Passover doorposts). LOGOS uses "mark" as conventional but notes the sign-reading is equally accurate. EMET and PNEUMA both prefer "sign" in their notes, though PNEUMA uses "mark" in the translation itself.

**SOPHIA rules:** The consensus uses **"mark"** in the running translation because it is the conventional English rendering that English readers immediately associate with this verse, but the Council Notes record that the Hebrew *'ot* is the covenant-sign word and bears none of the stigma or shame-connotation that "mark" carries in English idiom. The LXX *σημεῖον* ("sign") confirms the sign-reading.

---

### 4:16 — "Presence of the LORD" vs. "face of YHWH"

EMET: "from the face of YHWH." LOGOS: "from the presence of the LORD." PNEUMA: "from the LORD's presence." The Hebrew *milifnei YHWH* (מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה) literally means "from before the face of the LORD." **SOPHIA rules:** *"from the presence of the LORD"* — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; this is idiomatic English that preserves the spatial/relational meaning without importing the awkward literalism of "face" when speaking of divine departure (contrast v. 14 where Cain's own speech says "from your face I shall be hidden" — there the word "face" is retained for the first person address).

---

### 4:17 — Enoch

All agents agree on "Enoch" as the English rendering of Hebrew חֲנוֹךְ (*Chanokh*, "dedicated/initiated"). The consensus follows.

---

### 4:23–24 — Lamech's Song: poetic formatting and the "seventy-sevenfold" question

All three agents recognize this as the oldest embedded poem in the Genesis narrative and format it as verse. The consensus preserves the verse-paragraph structure.

**Poetic rendering decisions:**

- "Give ear to my speech" (LOGOS) over "give ear to my utterance" (EMET) or "listen to what I say" (PNEUMA) — LOGOS captures the formal register of *'imrati* (utterance/saying) without EMET's slightly archaic "utterance" or PNEUMA's colloquial looseness.
- "For a man I have killed for my wound, / and a young man for my bruise" — all three agents agree on this rendering. The ambiguity of *le-fits'i* ("for/because of my wound") is deliberately preserved: it may mean self-defense (because he wounded me) or at personal cost (at the price of my wound). The consensus does not resolve what the text does not resolve.

**The 77 vs. 490 question:** The MT reads *shiv'im ve-shiv'ah* (שִׁבְעִים וְשִׁבְעָה) — "seventy and seven" = 77. The LXX reads *ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά* — "seventy times seven" = 490. LOGOS notes that Jesus in Matthew 18:22 uses the LXX's phrasing in reverse (forgiveness, not vengeance). **SOPHIA rules:** The MT is followed — **"seventy-sevenfold"** — which represents 77 in the MT tradition. The LXX variant and the Matthew intertextuality are documented in the Disagreement Log.

---

### 4:25 — "The man" and Eve's naming speech

The Hebrew of v. 25 uses *adam* (אָדָם) without the definite article — shifting from "the man" (*ha-adam*) of earlier chapters toward the personal name Adam. LOGOS renders "Adam" here; EMET renders "the man." **SOPHIA rules:** *"the man"* is retained — the article's absence in the Hebrew is ambiguous and the shift toward the personal name Adam is better registered in Chapter 5's toledot formula. Consistency with prior chapters' rendering of *adam* as "the man" is preserved.

Eve's speech: "God has appointed for me another offspring in place of Abel, for Cain killed him." The plain naming of the murder at this point — Eve speaking it directly for the first time — is preserved exactly as in the MT.

---

### 4:26 — The *huchal* crux: "begun" vs. "profaned"

The Hebrew: אָז הוּחַל לִקְרֹא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה (*'az huchal liqro' beshem YHWH*).

The Hophal of חלל (*huchal*) is the key. Two readings:

1. **חלל II = "to begin"** — the standard reading; *huchal* = "it was begun." This is the reading of the MT with the Masoretic vocalization, the received reading, and what LOGOS, EMET, and PNEUMA all follow in their translations.

2. **חלל I = "to profane / to desecrate"** — an ancient minority reading preserved in Targum Onqelos ("then the sons of men began to call their idols by the name of the LORD") and known to Maimonides. On this reading, the verse marks the beginning of idolatry, not worship.

3. **The LXX** reads *οὗτος ἤλπισεν ἐπικαλεῖσθαι τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ* — "this one hoped to call upon the name of the LORD God" — reading *huchal* as a form of יחל ("to hope/wait") rather than חלל, with a personal subject (Enosh) rather than an impersonal passive.

**SOPHIA rules:** The consensus follows the MT's received reading — *"At that time it was begun to call upon the name of the LORD."* The impersonal passive is retained without inserting a subject ("people began"), because the grammar of the Hebrew does not specify who began; the openness is the text's own. This is the chapter's closing note, placed against the backdrop of Lamech's sword-song: the descent through murder, escalating vengeance, and the boast of unlimited retribution closes with the first prayer. The profanation reading (Targum Onqelos) and the LXX variant (hope/Enosh) are both documented in the Disagreement Log.

---

## Disagreement Log

### GEN 4:1 — *et*-YHWH: preposition or direct-object marker?

| Agent | Rendering | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I have acquired a man with/[by means of] YHWH" | Preserves both readings via slash notation |
| LOGOS | "I have gotten a man with the LORD" | Prepositional reading; follows LXX *διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ* |
| PNEUMA | "I have acquired a man — with the LORD" | Prepositional reading; em dash marks the declaration's force |
| **SOPHIA (consensus)** | **"I have acquired a man — with the LORD"** | **PNEUMA's phrasing adopted; prepositional reading followed** |

**Minority reading preserved:** The direct-object reading — "I have acquired a man — YHWH" (Aquila: *ἐκτησάμην ἄνθρωπον τὸν κύριον*) — is a live, ancient, and grammatically possible alternative. The Hebrew does not grammatically exclude it. It represents one of the earliest potential messianic readings embedded in the text and has been championed in modern scholarship. The consensus does not adopt it for the main text but affirms its validity as a reading that must not be dismissed.

---

### GEN 4:7 — LXX variant: total restructuring

| Witness | Text |
|---|---|
| MT (Leningrad Codex) | "Is it not so — if you do well, there is a lifting up? But if you do not do well, sin is crouching at the door. Its desire is for you — but you must rule over it." |
| LXX (Vaticanus) | "Is it not so that if you offer rightly, but do not divide rightly, you have sinned? Be still. Its turning is toward you, and you shall rule over it." |

The LXX eliminates the crouching-sin image entirely, imports a cultic interpretation ("offer rightly / divide rightly"), and adds the command "Be still" (*ἡσύχασον*). This is a substantial divergence, almost certainly reflecting the LXX translator's interpretive response to the broken syntax of the MT rather than a different Vorlage. The MT is followed. The LXX variant is evidence of the verse's notorious difficulty.

---

### GEN 4:8 — Missing speech: MT lacuna vs. LXX/Samaritan supply

| Witness | Text |
|---|---|
| MT (Leningrad Codex) | "And Cain said to Abel his brother." [no content] |
| LXX (Vaticanus) | "And Cain said to Abel his brother, 'Let us go out into the field.'" |
| Samaritan Pentateuch | "And Cain said to his brother Abel, 'Let us go out to the field.'" |
| Peshitta / Vulgate | Supply the speech similarly |
| PNEUMA | Follows LXX/Samaritan: includes "Let us go out to the field" |
| EMET, LOGOS | Follow MT: preserve the lacuna |
| **SOPHIA (consensus)** | **Follows MT: lacuna preserved** |

**Note:** The supplied speech ("Let us go out to the field") was almost certainly original and lost by haplography — the scribe's eye jumped from *achiv* to *vayyehi*. The LXX/Samaritan reading is the likely original. Nevertheless, the MT's *lectio difficilior* is the text this translation series follows, and the supply belongs in the textual apparatus. PNEUMA's decision to fill the gap is understandable on literary grounds but represents an editorial insertion not warranted by the MT.

---

### GEN 4:13 — *'avon*: punishment or iniquity?

| Agent | Rendering | Reading of *'avon* | Reading of *min-neso'* |
|---|---|---|---|
| EMET | "My iniquity/punishment is too great to bear/be-forgiven" | Both | Both |
| LOGOS | "My iniquity is too great to bear" | Moral guilt | Physical burden |
| PNEUMA | "My punishment is too great to carry" | Judicial sentence | Physical/emotional burden |
| **SOPHIA (consensus)** | **"My punishment is too great to bear"** | **Judicial sentence** | **Physical burden** |
| LXX (Vaticanus) | "My guilt/charge is too great to be forgiven" | Moral guilt | Forgiveness |

**SOPHIA's assessment:** The Hebrew עָוֹן (*'avon*) and the infinitive נְשֹׂא (*neso'*) are genuinely and permanently ambiguous. No translation can fully resolve this without loss. The consensus chooses "punishment / bear" as the contextually dominant reading; the guilt/forgiveness axis (LOGOS, LXX) is an equally valid reading. The verse is polyvalent by design: Cain the sinner is complaining about his sentence while perhaps, at some depth, acknowledging the weight of his deed. The slash notation of EMET most honestly represents the Hebrew but cannot stand in a fluid consensus translation.

---

### GEN 4:23–24 — Lamech's Song: 77 vs. 490

| Witness | Number |
|---|---|
| MT (שִׁבְעִים וְשִׁבְעָה) | 77 ("seventy and seven") |
| LXX (ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά) | 490 ("seventy times seven") |
| Matthew 18:22 (NT) | Uses LXX phrasing for forgiveness |
| **SOPHIA (consensus)** | **"seventy-sevenfold" = 77, following MT** |

The LXX variant (490) would make the NT allusion in Matthew 18:22 even more pointed — Jesus exactly inverting Lamech's number as the measure of forgiveness rather than vengeance. Whether Matthew intends the MT's 77 or the LXX's 490 is a NT question that lies beyond the scope of this translation. The MT is followed.

---

### GEN 4:26 — *huchal*: "begun" vs. "profaned" vs. LXX "hoped"

| Witness | Reading | Theological import |
|---|---|---|
| MT (הוּחַל, Hophal of חלל II) | "it was begun" | Beginning of YHWH-worship |
| Targum Onqelos | "then the sons of men began to call their idols by the name of YHWH" | Beginning of idolatry / profanation |
| LXX Vaticanus (ἤλπισεν) | "this one [Enosh] hoped to call upon the name of the LORD God" | Personal, eschatological hope; different verb entirely (יחל not חלל) |
| EMET | "Then [it was] begun [lit. it was profaned] to call on the name of YHWH" | Preserves both by parenthetical note |
| LOGOS | "At that time it was begun to call upon the name of the LORD" | Standard MT reading |
| PNEUMA | "At that time, people began to call on the name of the LORD" | MT reading; adds personal subject "people" |
| **SOPHIA (consensus)** | **"At that time it was begun to call upon the name of the LORD"** | **MT received reading; impersonal passive preserved** |

**SOPHIA's note:** PNEUMA's addition of "people" as a subject smooths the Hebrew passive but inserts a word not in the MT. The impersonal passive — *"it was begun"* — without a specified subject is the text's own ambiguity: who began? The grammar does not say. This openness at the close of chapter 4 is appropriate. The profanation reading (Targum) is a profound and ancient alternative that surfaces the tension between the chapter's descent and its close; the MT's vocalization does not support it, but the Targum tradition that preserves it should not be dismissed as merely tendentious.

---
*Consensus translation and arbiter notes by SOPHIA (Σοφία). All primary readings from Leningrad Codex B19A, Folios 3r–3v (West Semitic Research Project / Bruce E. Zuckerman photographs), cross-referenced with Codex Vaticanus Vat.gr.1209 (LXX). Council of translators: EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic).*
